intro: Argentina's rental market operates under specific regulations (including DNU 70/2023), standard indexing methods like the BCRA's ICL, real estate commission conventions, and recurring expenses such as [building maintenance fees](/calculadora-expensas-departamento-calcular-m2-categoria). Choosing between a 2-year and 3-year lease involves weighing stability against flexibility. This calculator compares both options, showing typical guarantees, rent adjustment methods, and the key advantages of each term length. All figures are current as of 2026 and reviewed regularly.

explanation:
Argentina's rental market is governed by the Civil and Commercial Code and modified by DNU 70/2023, which changed earlier rental regulations. Both landlords and tenants have significant freedom to negotiate lease terms, currency, and adjustment methods.

## How the Comparison Works

This calculator displays the typical guarantees, adjustment methods, and specific advantages for 2-year vs 3-year leases based on current Argentine rental market practices. The tool helps you understand the trade-offs between each option and flags any unusual terms outside typical ranges.

## Important Notes

This calculator provides general guidance only. For binding legal, financial, or contractual decisions, consult with a licensed real estate attorney or rental specialist. All information is current as of 2026 and regularly reviewed.

faq.q: What's the main difference between a 2-year and 3-year lease?
faq.a: A 2-year lease offers more flexibility and the chance to renegotiate every 2 years, while a 3-year lease provides longer-term stability and more predictable rent increases. Your choice depends on market conditions and personal housing stability needs.

faq.q: Are 2-year leases legal in Argentina in 2026?
faq.a: Yes. Following DNU 70/2023, lease terms are now freely negotiable. You can choose 2-year, 3-year, or custom terms by mutual agreement with the landlord.

faq.q: What types of guarantees do landlords typically require?
faq.a: Common options include a property guarantee (a guarantor with real estate backing), surety insurance, salary documentation (typically 3x monthly rent), or a bank guarantee bond. Requirements vary and are negotiable.

faq.q: How are rent increases typically handled on leases?
faq.a: Under DNU 70/2023, parties can agree on any adjustment method: CVS, USD, MEP dollar, ICL (BCRA's inflation index), UVA, or other agreed indices. This is a freely negotiable term.

faq.q: Can I end a lease early before the term expires?
faq.a: Yes, but it usually requires notice and compensation. Typical terms are 1 month's rent if terminated in year one, or 1.5 months if after year one. Exact terms must be stated in the contract.

faq.q: Why is a move-in inspection so important?
faq.a: A signed inspection report with photos is critical to document the property's condition on day one. This protects both tenant and landlord by providing evidence if disputes arise at lease end.

faq.q: Which lease term offers better long-term financial savings?
faq.a: A 3-year lease locks in rent adjustments, providing cost predictability. A 2-year lease lets you renegotiate sooner if market rates fall. The best choice depends on rent trends and your financial flexibility.

faq.q: What costs beyond base rent should I expect?
faq.a: Common additional costs include building maintenance fees (expensas), property taxes, utilities, internet, and renters insurance. Some expenses are tenant-paid, others landlord-paid—these are all negotiable terms.
